blocks.content: The Hailey house was a particular kind of quiet on a Saturday morning — not empty, just still. Thomas Hailey answered the door in a cardigan and reading glasses and looked at Ethan with the measured gaze of a man who had been expecting this visit, if not today then soon.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Mr. Hailey. Thank you for having me.

The living room had the same quality as Cassandra's apartment — things chosen carefully, arranged for comfort rather than display. A stack of books on the side table. A photograph on the mantle that Ethan recognized: a young Cassandra and a younger Maya at a beach somewhere, both of them squinting into the sun.

Mr. Hailey sat across from him in the chair that was clearly his — the armrests worn at the exact places his elbows went — and folded his hands in his lap and looked at Ethan without speaking. Not unfriendly. Just: measuring.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I want to marry your daughter, sir. I know that's why I'm here and I didn't want to work up to it. I wanted to say it plainly so you'd know where I stand from the beginning.
Thomas Hailey looked at him for a long moment.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Hailey
blocks.content: Tell me something, Ethan. Tell me that you understand what you're signing up for. Not the idea of it. The actual weight of it.
